data.title: Energy Transfer: 8% Yield, 1.7X Coverage, Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) is growing its asset footprint in the vital Permian Basin through the expansion of the Transwestern Pipeline with which the MLP seeks to serve the growing markets in Arizona and New Mexico. Further, the midstream platform generated considerable growth in its distributable cash flow in the second quarter to which the expansion project in the Permian is most likely going to add.

I like that Energy Transfer is one of the cheapest plays in the midstream market, which I consider to be quite undeserved. With an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of only 8.4X, I consider ET to be one of the most attractive midstream enterprises in the market. Since Energy Transfer is also growing its distribution, I believe the risk profile is highly favorable for investors.

Previous rating

I rated units of Energy Transfer a strong buy in my last coverage in June -- A Top MLP Investment Pick -- because I liked the midstream platform’s asset positioning in the Permian as well as the MLP's strong distribution coverage profile. In the second fiscal quarter, Energy Transfer announced a distribution increase to $0.33 per-unit and the midstream enterprise reiterated its aggressive growth CapEx of $5.0B which includes investments in the critical Desert Southwest project.

Core value proposition for income investors

Energy Transfer is a leading midstream platform with a focus on large-scale, long-haul pipelines. The energy enterprise is one of the largest midstream companies in North America and has an especially strong pipeline footprint in the Permian Basin. Energy Transfer utilizes its more than 140,000-mile-long pipeline network for the transport of energy raw materials, which include natural gas, natural gas liquids, crude oil and refined products. Besides pipelines, Energy Transfer owns an array of other transportation, storage and fractionation facilities that are needed in order to transport energy to consumer end-markets, as well as export facilities along the U.S. Gulf Coast.

The main appeal of an investment in Energy Transfer comes from the fact that the energy company signs long-term supply contracts with its producer clients, which are mostly fee-based. For the current fiscal year, for instance, ET expects to generate 90% of its adjusted EBITDA to come from fees, which are highly predictable as they are determined in advance.

Energy Transfer has specific potential to grow its EBITDA and distributable cash flow through the “Desert Southwest” expansion project, which is a project meant to increase the reach of the Transwestern Pipeline. The Transwestern Pipeline links the Permian Basin in West Texas with the fast-growing markets in Arizona and New Mexico. The Desert Southwest Pipeline expansion at its core is a 516-mile long natural gas pipeline connecting the Permian Basin to Arizona and targeting a transport capacity of 1.5 billion cubic feet per day.

This expansion project is to be seen in the context of ET pursuing an aggressive CapEx program that is meant to

  1. Strengthen the firm’s positioning, especially in the Permian,
  2. Add incremental pipeline capacity to broaden the reach of ET’s energy distribution, and
  3. Support Energy Transfer’s organic distributable cash flow growth.

The midstream enterprise guided for $5.0B in capital expenditures just this year, with management saying that a “significant amount” of this CapEx will go to projects with links to the Permian Basin.

Energy Transfer has routinely relied on acquisitions to grow, and the MLP pumped billions of dollars into organic growth projects. Despite this aggressive growth CapEx program, ET has no issues supporting its distribution with distributable cash flow. In fact, the distribution coverage ratio in Q2'25 was a robust 1.7X, which was about on the same level as Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD)'s coverage (EPD had a distribution coverage ratio of 1.6X in the second quarter). Because of this comfortable excess coverage, ET has been able to consistently raise its distribution. The current (Q2'25) distribution rate is $0.33/unit, which shows a total 3% rise compared to the year-earlier period.

$millions Q2'24 Q3'24 Q4'24 Q1'25 Q2'25 Y/Y Growth
Adjusted EBITDA $3,760 $3,959 $3,884 $4,098 $3,866 3%
Distributable Cash Flow For ET Partners $2,038 $1,975 $1,974 $2,305 $1,958 -4%
Transaction Adjustments $1 $15 $4 $2 $1 0%
Adjusted Distributable Cash Flow $2,039 $1,990 $1,978 $2,307 $1,959 -4%
Distributions (Limited And General) $1,096 $1,105 $1,116 $1,125 $1,134 3%
Distribution Coverage Ratio 1.86X 1.80X 1.77X 2.05X 1.73X -7%

Source: Author

Energy Transfer’s valuation

The units of Energy Transfer are one of the most affordable in the MLP industry, and I don’t believe that there is a good reason for this: ET is currently valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 8.4X, which is about 19% below the industry group average EBITDA multiplier of 10.4X.

Enterprise Products Partners, Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI), and Enbridge Inc. (ENB), all major players in the North American midstream sector, are trading at enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratios of 10.1X, 11.5X, and 13.0X. As I have hinted at in my previous work on companies in the midstream sector, Enterprise Products Partners is my all-time favorite MLP given its 27-year consistent history of distribution growth.

In my opinion, units of Energy Transfer could easily revalue to an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10-11X, especially because the company is splurging on new income-generating projects, especially in natural gas and with geographic concentration in the Permian Basin. This valuation range translates into a fair value for units of Energy Transfer between $20.73 and $22.79 per unit.

Risks with Energy Transfer

The biggest risk for Energy Transfer, as I see it, relates to a potential slowdown in the natural gas business, which may slow down certain capital projects, such as the Desert Southwest expansion project. However, Energy Transfer has performed well over a very long period of time and should be able to grow its EBITDA and distributable cash flow in the long term. What may change my view on the energy company is if it experiences a drastic deterioration of its distribution coverage profile.

Closing thoughts

Energy Transfer makes a compelling value proposition on a number of dimensions, including:

  1. An aggressive capital framework with a tilt towards the Transwestern pipeline connecting the Permian Basin to markets in Arizona and New Mexico,
  2. Solid EBITDA growth, backed by past acquisitions and organic growth projects, and
  3. Strong distribution coverage.

What I like most about Energy Transfer, however, is that the midstream platform is trading at a very compelling EV-to-EBITDA ratio compared to other midstream companies. Since ET is also growing its distribution and has very healthy excess coverage, plus a reiterated $5.0B growth CapEx program, I believe Energy Transfer remains a strong buy for dividend investors.

data.title: Nvidia's Strong AI Moat Justifies The Premium
data.description:
Since my last coverage on Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) last month, the stock has been almost flat due to several reasons, mainly due to the news of China's chip halts. However, I believe that the chip giant is positioned in the center of the AI compute wave, and that is the only reason to have a bullish tilt. It is not only a company that sells chips, but it also sells the compute layer that enables generation models of today and predictions to which the software and ecosystem benefits are sticky and cannot be easily copied by its rivals.

Yes, I know that bears will say what about its expensiveness, so to satisfy them, I would say yes, I agree that the stock price is aggressively growing, but the revenue and margin trend that Nvidia has taken to date has been equally predictable. In my opinion, Nvidia's growth justifies a premium valuation.

Moreover, among record incomes and such collaborations as it did recently with Intel (INTC) and OpenAI, Nvidia can be expected to continue its AI moat. I maintain my rating of the stock as a "Buy" based on the premise that the advanced chips designed by Nvidia will be critical to the expansion of AI-based artificial intelligence, and that the recent transactions will enhance its dominance. Financials are also doing great with Revenue and Income continuously rising in double digits.

I believe that the Chip Giant Nvidia stands at the center of the AI boom, and that should fuel the share to a greater level.

OpenAI Partnership: $100B Strategic Bet

Recently, Nvidia stunned Wall Street by announcing a mega partnership with OpenAI. According to this deal, the chipmaker will deploy at least 10 gigawatts of GPU-powered data center capacity for OpenAI’s next-gen models and invest up to $100B in OpenAI as each phase is rolled out.

This actually means that Nvidia is funding the AI bubble that it's attempting to create. The initial cash commitment of $10 billion is invested to supply the powered capacity of a gigawatt with the new Vera Rubin chips.

In other words, we can say that, by partnering directly with OpenAI, Nvidia assures the existence of a huge future for its GPUs; OpenAI's entire stack will run almost exclusively on Nvidia systems. The deal also gives Nvidia a stake in the growth of OpenAI, though it gives them no voting rights.

In the long term, any profits from that $100 billion pot depend on OpenAI’s evolution, but the short-term upside is clear: guaranteed demand for Nvidia’s H100/Blackwell chips and likely co-development of optimized hardware.

Factually, this OpenAI deal has changed the way people are ordering Nvidia data-center graphics cards. It goes beyond headline capital; it guarantees the multi-year consumption of top-notch chips, which other users follow. Plus, there is the not-so-subtle upside that because it ties hardware and models into each other, Nvidia tightens its moat, making it harder to switch. And here, further to the previously mentioned caveat, the risk lies in concentration if OpenAI shifts tech or develops proprietary acceleration, then that upside might be lost.

Yet, the duo leaves Apple (AAPL) at the bottom of the technology resource line because, for better or worse, Nvidia is the king of next-generation generative AI scaling and, arguably, the rightful and wisely driven owner of that technology for the time being.

Intel Collaboration: Two Giants Unite

Elsewhere, Nvidia has struck a deal with Intel to build NAI next-gen chips. The two announced creating fused versions of their world-leading platforms in September 2025. Nvidia is pouring in a billion and a half of its wealth into Intel shares, and they are collaborating to jointly design a proprietary CPU coupled with GPU-built-in PC chips. Intel will design x86 CPUs that would perfectly fit into Nvidia platforms, whereas Nvidia graphics chips will be integrated into the new Intel PC systems-on-a-chip. This is a win-win partnership for Nvidia on various levels.

To begin with, it opens their ecosystem up: Nvidia stacks will be executing on Intel manufacturing and architecture, taking the brand into additional markets (particularly PCs).

Second, a new investment in Intel would strengthen the relationship between the U.S. chip industry and would dampen any supply dwarfism.

Lastly, it puts Nvidia in a better position to compete with other global markets: collaborating, Nvidia and Intel will be able to match such players under the same conditions as ARM-based solutions, and companies can compete with either their own systems.

According to CEO Jensen Huang, "The combination of the AI stack developed by Nvidia with the Intel CPUs is set to underpin the next generation of computers." In the company's own words, these are basically Nvidia in a larger portion of the future equipment.

It is practical: it will open even more market outlets (PCs, data centers), but alleviate supply pressure. In the case of Nvidia, the acquisition will result in their accelerators being a co-processor, rather than an add-on. It also alleges a power struggle, politically and economically, strengthening the influence of the U.S. chips against external competitors.

But remember execution matters. The relationship must literally implement joint product roadmaps and follow the manufacturing schedules. When it happens, Nvidia may enter into an array of markets with fine margins, and when it does not, the business press is doing little, but giving premium exposure to a fictional business result, with a commercial gain case nonexistent.

Chinese Competition and Regulatory Challenge

Having said that, it's not all sunshine and roses for Nvidia; there's a whole other set of challenges out there in China, which is the world's second-biggest AI market. Chinese firms, led by Huawei, are racing to develop their own AI chips and their own supernodes. That includes Huawei's new Ascend chips and the upcoming "Atlas" supercomputers, which they're doing their darndest to sell as fast as Nvidia's stuff. At the same time, Chinese regulators have hit Nvidia with charges that the company is engaging in anti-competitive behavior, and issued an order to companies to refrain from purchasing Nvidia's high-end AI GPUs.

In fact, it is only days after Nvidia AI chips were considered that regulators issued a heads-up to Chinese tech firms to avoid the chips, in part to flex local muscle. All of this ensures an increased risk that some of the demand generated by Chinese cloud providers could dry up.

Still, even the people inside Huawei admit that Nvidia's silicon is hard to beat at the moment. Analysts note that China's move forward notwithstanding, Nvidia's GPUs maintain their dominance in the raw performance and software ecosystem.

If China sticks to its own chip path, Analysts say that

"It would be possible that China splits the market, with Nvidia, who would pursue the non-Chinese hyperscalers, and the Chinese clouds would stick it to chips made in China."

For investors, that means a short-term risk (nipping out of the market share in China) but also a reminder that Nvidia's tech is still irreplaceable (China gets nervous enough to fight back).

Investors are watching the U.S.-China trade relations quite closely, but the fact that Nvidia has such a diverse base of customers, ranging from OpenAI to cloud providers to automotive and many others, means that the ability Nvidia partners with gives it a lot of diversification.

To sum it up, I would say that in any case, the moat Nvidia has built is solid, even as other players are trying to catch up to it.

Stellar Financial Growth

Nvidia’s financial has been great with rapidly growing its top and bottom line. Over the last five years revenue has skyrocketed from $10.9B in 2019 to $27B in 2022, then doubled to $60.9B in 2024.

Net income exploded even faster: GAAP net profit in FY2024 was about $29.8B, up 581% from $4.4B in FY2023.

Nothing different happened here. Operating cash flow tripled to $28.1B in FY2024 versus $5.6B the year before. Key contributors are obvious: data center (AI) revenue has grown 400%+ year-over-year recently, and gaming has rebounded as RTX GPUs, now also AI inference engines, attract more users.

Here is a little breakdown:

  • Five-Year Trend: Revenue has been up 6x since 2018; EPS has been up even more.

  • Recent Quarters: Q2 FY2025 (ended Oct ’24) saw record $46B revenue (up 6% from Q2, +56% YoY). Data Center sales led the way.

  • Cash Returns: Nvidia has used its surplus cash to buy back stock (already $25B repurchased in H1 FY2025) and hiked dividends also.

Along with these great numbers from the past, the management is optimistic about the future. Since then, every quarterly earnings call has shown that AI demand is picking up.

Nvidia is expecting to see Q3 revenue (ending October) come in at approximately $54B (+-2%) with AI and data-center workloads remaining strong. They are running around 73% GAAP/non-GAAP gross margins, and intend to end the year in the mid-70s with non-GAAP as well.

Management estimated operating expenses in the range of $5.9B (GAAP) / $4.2B (non-GAAP) in the quarter.

The rules are visible from the guidance, which clearly assumes no H20 shipments to China, and execs said a change in policy could unlock an additional $2B to $5B in H20 revenue.

Even analysts are reviving their figures: expect revenues to exceed $100B in FY2025, up from $60.9B in FY2024.

If things clear up in the technology industry, Nvidia's performance in the past couple of quarters has been nice, thanks to all sorts of top-of-the-field forecasts. All in all, the company's cash runway is rock solid also.

So all I can say is that Nvidia's financials will speak loud and clear: exceptional revenue growth, widening margins, and tons of operating cash flow. The company successfully transformed from the manufacturer of graphics cards mainly used for games into the underlying platform of artificial intelligence infrastructure, which turned out to be extremely profitable. Nvidia's growth is not an illusion; it has actual structural demand.

Rich Valuation Amid Fast Growth

Valuation of Nvidia is one issue that remains in my mind and rest easy but I have my reasoning with why Nvidia has high multiples and explain why it will make sense to pay a superior price to Nvidia.

At this stage, the prospective measures are fairly balanced in all respects, and its P/FCF ratio is elevated also. As an example, NVDA has a forward P/E of 39 and Its price earnings ratio is approximately 60 times.

Compared to the big players, they are also rich: Broadcom (AVGO) has a price to forward P/E at 40x, price to book to forward EPS at 64, and price to forward capital cost of 64 and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has a price to forward P/E of 32 and a price to forward book correlation of 64x as well as a price to forward capital cost of 64.

Nvidia has an estimated trailing EPS of approximately $3.5, the share prices trade at a forward P/E in the range of 39 times. The current valuation is associated with that price suggesting that investors will want to see the company grow by about 18% per annum in EPS.

According to analysts, the Nvidia EPS has been projected to increase to over $4.50 in Jan 2026, reach over $6.34 in Jan 2027, and $7.44 in Jan 2028, implying an annual growth of a projected 50 percent, 41 percent, and 17 percent, correspondingly. Diluted P/Es increase with an increase in earnings to 39.5, 28.0, and 23.9. These forecasts confirm the existence of a high multiple: the rate of increasing EPS can justify the existing valuation as fulfilled over time.

Thus, although the value of Nvidia is high, this is not excessively large in comparison with the peers who have received an AI growth narrative. Further on, the PEG of Nvidia over the next 5 years stands at 0.79x or less than 1, which implies that analysts project that the earnings would support the stock price.

Key Risks

There are several risks that Nvidia is facing as of now:

Geopolitical: China's ban on sophisticated chips is real. The Chinese leadership only recently ordered local companies to cease making certain Nvidia GPU purchases, as well as initiate an antitrust investigation. In addition, U.S. export controls are squeezing the background of the sale of Nvidia in China. Should the U.S.-China tech war escalate, it can devour a slice of demand; however, at this stage, China only gets a smaller slice of the pie.

Competition: Huawei is a Chinese manufacturer that is on a rapid pace with their AI chips, and AMD and Intel have been asserting their position with the MI300 and Gaudi/Xeon. Nvidia may be forced to reduce its high-end pricing in an event where one of them pulls first.

Valuation Adjustment: Nvidia is susceptible to a technology sell off. A more far-reaching downturn in semiconductors or lack of management may spark a drastic drawback.

Concentration: The largest amount of Nvidia growth relies on big cloud players, OpenAI and Microsoft in addition to a few others. The abrupt change in their order or a replacement with in-house solutions would be detrimental.

Nvidia has not been erased of these threats, but these are benefits to consider. The most timely regulatory headwind can be perceived in the China/antitrust issue.

Conclusion: Maintain “Buy”

Therefore, I am fairly certain that Nvidia remains the industry leader in the semiconductor business. Nvidia is supported by the majority of social media, breaking down more than $100 billion of investments in Gen AI, and the figures are continuously crushing the company. Fancy having a bearish story of that.

Literally, the company is on fire, its roadmap is sound, and the collaborations with OpenAI, Intel, cloud service providers, etc., indicate that we are not off the runway as yet, and it has already been multiplied several times.

Admittedly, its price is high, yet a truckful of the AI chips and an expanding ecosystem that is keeping the things stable makes this high price worthwhile. I have got a Buy on NVDA, and I believe that the future earnings are bound to continue skyrocketing with the world becoming fully accelerated computerized.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Navigating GLP Controversy With Aplomb, But Risks Warrant Hold Rating
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) in early August, analyzing its Q2 earnings – here is the summary I provided:

Hims & Hers reported revenues of $544.8m ($537m of online revenue, $8m of wholesale revenue), up 73% year-on-year. Net income was reported as $42.5m (margin of ~8%), up from $13.3m in Q2 2024, while adjusted EDITDA amounted to $82.2m (margin of ~15%), up from $39.3m.

The number of subscribers to the Hims & Hers platform rose to 2.4m, up 31% year-on-year. Monthly online revenue per subscriber increased from $57 in Q2 2024, to $74, up 30% (but was reportedly down on a sequential basis, from >$80). Net cash used in operating activities came to $(19.1m), versus $53.6m in the prior year period, while free cashflow amounted to $(69.4m), versus $47.6m in the equivalent period last year.

Hims & Hers says it expects Q3 2025 revenues to come to $570m - $590m, with adjusted EBITDA of $60m - $70m (margin of 11-12%), and for the full-year 2025, revenues are expected to be $2.3bn - $2.4bn, adjusted EBITDA $295m - $335m(margin of 13-14%).

Hims & Hers reported a cash position of $1.12bn, compared to $221m in the prior year quarter, with current total liabilities of $(272m), and $(969.5m) of convertible senior notes outstanding.

At that time, Hims & Hers stock traded at $56 per share, and today, it trades at $57 per share, although during this short period, stock sank as low as $42 in early September before climbing steadily back to parity. The current market cap valuation is $12.91bn.

Readers will doubtless be aware that Hims operates a health technology platform that offers data-driven personalized treatment plans to members. In a recent corporate presentation, the company describes a "virtuous circle" whereby the more patients it attracts to its platform, the more data it can capture, to create better and more personalized solutions and higher quality outcomes, attracting more subscribers.

Hims & Hers is also a business that has courted controversy, however, thanks to its policy of offering "compounded" versions of GLP-1 receptor agonist drugs to patients. The GLP-1 class of drugs has been made famous by the "miracle" weight loss drugs Wegovy (semaglutide) and Zepbound (tirzepatide), developed by Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY), respectively. They are also approved as Ozempic and Mounjaro, respectively, in the indication of Type 2 diabetes ("T2D"), and in Q2 alone, total semaglutide revenues were >$8bn, and total tirzepatide revenues were $8.6bn.

Hims first announced it was entering the compounded GLP market in May last year – compounded versions of drugs are non-FDA-approved versions that may be sold under certain circumstances, for example, if there is a global shortage of a drug, or if an FDA-approved drug is not medically appropriate to treat them.

Hims began by capitalizing on a global shortage of semaglutide to manufacture and sell its own compounded version, which outraged Novo Nordisk. When the shortage was declared over earlier this year, Novo and Hims struck a deal for Hims to sell Wegovy via its platform, but Novo swiftly pulled out when it discovered Hims continued to offer a "personalized" version of Wegovy at a dose Novo did not offer (arguing that Novo's doses were not medically appropriate for some patients).

This has led to some difficult questions for investors to mull over. How much of Hims' success is down to its GLP offering? If Novo and Lilly get their way and compounded GLP drugs are outlawed, would shares suffer a major downward correction? How likely is a ban on compounding GLP's?

What certainly seems to be clear is that Hims' share price displays significant volatility and is generally sensitive to breaking news and new developments; therefore, in the rest of this post, I will turn my attention to a few pieces of relevant news flow that have occurred since my last note, and speculate about how they may affect Hims' business long term and what that may mean for its long-term share price performance.

Hims Under Fire - Business Practices Under The Microscope

I want to focus on four pieces of news as reported by Seeking Alpha since my note. The first I'd consider relatively minor – on August 12th, the company's CEO, Andrew Dudum, disclosed the sale of ~$33m, or ~669k shares of his Hims stock holding – ~7% of his entire stake.

The market often interprets insider selling negatively, as a sign that insiders might know something outsiders don't, and are selling in anticipation that stock might be about to crash. In this instance, given the amount sold was only 7% of the CEO's stock, if I were a shareholder in the company, this would not concern me.

The second piece of news is that the Federal Trade Commission is apparently investigating Hims for unfair trade practices, having received complaints from consumers about whether the company was making it too hard to cancel subscriptions.

In its corporate presentation, Hims notes that "our recurring revenue model sets a foundation for strong revenue growth and margin expansion," and says recurring revenue is >90%, with a 31% annual growth in subscribers in Q2 2025, although as noted above, that figure actually fell sequentially.

If subscribers are in fact wanting off the platform, then Hims' virtuous circle is at risk of becoming a vicious circle of ever diminishing returns, so in that sense, the news may be interpreted as somewhat troubling, although the company itself recently told its in-house investing community that:

We’ve seen a rehashed story from Bloomberg about an ongoing FTC inquiry. This is the same inquiry as last year, and as we said then, we support the FTC’s mission to protect consumers and remain committed to best practices in everything we do. We are voluntarily cooperating and providing information in response.

As we can see above, HIMS says it has 0.9m non-personalized subscribers, and 1.5m personalized subscribers – up 89% per annum overall—and that monthly average revenue per subscriber has been growing, apart from last quarter, as mentioned.

A bear thesis worth discussing could be that Hims has attracted so many subscribers in the past year due to its offering GLP-1 drugs. It's hard to know if the bulk of those were hoping to buy semaglutide itself, or were attracted by the promise of buying a cheaper compounded drug?

Either scenario is potentially problematic for Hims, because in the former, patients no longer have access to semaglutide (or tirzepatide) via the platform, and in the latter, if the "Big Pharmas," Novo and Lilly, are successful in their attempts to have compounding GLPs outlawed, then more and more patients may want to leave.

"Compounding" (excuse the pun) the FTC investigation issue is the news that a Super Bowl ad commissioned by Hims met with criticism from none other than the Commissioner of the FDA, Dr. Marty Makary. The commissioner wrote in an op-ed piece that:

Online pharmacies are advertising drugs with only upsides mentioned, which can contribute to America’s pill-popping culture.

This breach of F.D.A. regulation was most overt earlier this year when Hims & Hers ran a Super Bowl ad parading the benefits of GLP-1 drugs without any mention of side effects

In fairness to Hims, the company shared data in July relating to its compounded GLP product as follows:

In a first-of-its-kind U.S. analysis of treatment plans, including compounded GLP-1s, customers taking a compounded GLP-1 reported losing an average of 20.9 pounds or 10.3% of initial body weight, within six months, while reporting a low rate of side effects. Just as importantly, 75% of customers remained on treatment plans in the same timeframe, a powerful signal of real-world trust and clinical success.

Only 10.3% reported side effects, and an even smaller 4.5% reported intolerable side effects. Among those who completed a six-month check-in, 97% of customers on plans with compounded GLP-1s reported an improvement in their overall health.

Whether Hims' data would stand up to the kind of scrutiny focused on drug trials for candidates seeking a formal FDA approval is open to question, but it's clear that the company has no plans to back down from selling compounded GLP. In some ways, if advertising is viewed as Hims' biggest problem by the FDA's commissioner, that could actually be a positive, as Hims can make its advertising less aggressive overnight. The fact that the FDA's commissioner does not call for compounding to be outlawed may indicate he is not necessarily opposed to it.

FDA's Stance On Compounding Analyzed

The fourth piece of news flow deals with the compounding issue. On 16th September, Seeking Alpha reported that the FDA had issued Hims with a warning letter concerning its marketing of compounded semaglutide. The FDA wrote that:

Compounded drug products are not FDA-approved. Your claims imply that your products are the same as an FDA-approved product when they are not. As a result, these claims are false or misleading and your products are therefore misbranded.

Hims responded by claiming that the FDA has sent letters to multiple companies, including Lilly and Novo, "as part of the administration’s recent push on pharmaceutical marketing."

Eli Lilly's CEO was critical of efforts being made by law enforcement and regulatory agencies to outlaw compounding during the company's recent Q2 earnings call, and the issue was also thought to be behind the decision to oust Novo's CEO earlier this year, as the Pharma blamed compounding for failing to meet the market's expectations on revenue and growth.

In my view, the critical point here is whether the FDA and wider government side with Eli Lilly and Novo, and move to outlaw compounding, or whether they continue to allow Hims (and others) to market and sell compounded semaglutide.

Analysis - What Is The Play On Hims Today?

Hims is a >$13bn market cap company today, that expects to earn $2.3bn–$2.4bn of revenues this year and, long-term, has set out expectations for $6.5bn of revenues in 2030, with $1.3bn of adjusted EBITDA.

If these goals are achieved, then the logical conclusion is that Hims stock price and valuation will rise. A $6.5bn revenue, profitable telehealth platform would warrant a >$20bn market cap valuation, I'd speculate, applying a ~3x price to sale ratio that is about average for the pharma sector. Factor in evidence of strong growth potential, and the valuation could be even higher.

However, we must acknowledge the risk that the FDA puts an increasing amount of pressure on Hims to stop selling compounded semaglutide. In my view, this risk is significant enough to merit a Hold recommendation on HIMS stock while we wait for more clarity.

Recent news flow arguably highlights the fact that Hims has been sailing close to the wind with its business practices, but as discussed, marketing strategy can be easily adjusted without necessarily impacting revenues and growth, but an end to its compounding business would be extremely damaging for Hims and its growth plans.

Looking further ahead, however, with the weight-loss industry maturing beyond GLP-1 drugs and into lean muscle mass preservation, combination therapies, etc., Hims today has an ample opportunity to add new products and services to its platform and grow in this way instead.

Hims has made some potentially dangerous enemies in Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk, but has managed to hold its own to date, which is a positive, and justifies its current valuation; however, the risks related to arguably its best known product are high enough to warrant a cautious outlook at this time, in my view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Okta: One Of The Best 'Growth At A Reasonable Price' Stocks To Buy
data.description:
With the stock market reeling from all-time highs, the large-cap tech stocks that have dominated this year's gains are beginning to fall out of favor. The notion that the stock market is expensive, however, ignores the fact that many small- and mid-cap stocks that have lived in the shadow of their large-cap peers have slid this year and are trading at excellent prices.

Okta, Inc. (NASDAQ:OKTA), in my view, is one of the best "growth at a reasonable price" stocks that money can buy right now. Shares are up a modest ~15% this year but have slid consistently since peaking near $130 in May, even though direct competitor CyberArk Software Ltd. (CYBR) recently got acquired for a hefty premium. In my view, it's a great time for investors to reassess the bull case for Okta.

I last wrote a buy note on Okta in July, when the stock was trading closer to $100 per share and when the Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) - CyberArk deal was just announced. Since then, shares of Okta have slid a further ~10%, while its latest Q2 results showcased a healthy growth trajectory. I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion here.

In my view, these are the top reasons to be long on Okta:

  • Okta addresses a massive $80 billion TAM. The company's end market is massive, as Okta is a truly horizontal software product that is applicable to customers across all industries.
  • High-margin recurring revenue base. 98% of Okta's revenue sits in recurring subscription revenue, which comes in at a high margin stream. This unlocks substantial economies of scale for Okta as it grows larger.
  • Rule of 40. The company's low-teens revenue growth plus its high-20s percent pro forma operating margins showcase a strong balance between growth and profitability, which further underscores a modest valuation multiple. Few companies can hit the "Rule of 40" status, let alone stocks that have fallen into the penalty box like Okta.
  • Clean balance sheet. Okta has about $2 billion of net cash on its books, which represents a hefty chunk of its current ~$16 billion market value. This ample liquidity gives Okta plenty of flexibility to pursue growth initiatives to address the recent deceleration.

To me, the single best reason to invest in Okta is its cheap valuation. At current share prices just below $90, Okta trades at a market cap of $15.95 billion. After we net off the $2.86 billion of cash and $859 million of convertible debt on Okta's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $13.95 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26, Okta has raised its full-year revenue outlook to $2.875-$2.885 billion in revenue, or 10%-11% y/y growth, which is one point higher than a previous 9%-10% y/y growth expectation. It's also expecting a 28% FCF margin ($806 million at the midpoint) against this revenue profile, which is one point richer than a previous 27% margin expectation.

For FY27, Wall Street analysts are expecting Okta to hit $3.15 billion in revenue, or 9% y/y growth. Considering the double-digit growth rate in Okta's RPO, I don't think the company is at risk of decelerating to ~9% growth anytime soon. But if we take Street estimates at face value and assume Okta can hold its 28% FCF margin through next year, FCF in FY27 would be $882 million.

This puts Okta's valuation multiples at:

  • 4.8x EV/FY26 revenue and 4.4x EV/FY27 revenue.
  • 17.3x EV/FY26 FCF and 15.8x EV/FY27 FCF.

To me, being able to buy into a mid-teens software growth stock that is a category leader in its space at a <5x forward revenue and mid-teens FCF multiple is an incredible bargain that is tough to find in today's market. Stay long here and use the near-term dip in this stock as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Okta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Okta's revenue grew 13% y/y to $728 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $716 million (+11% y/y) by a two-point margin. We note as well that Okta's revenue growth accelerated slightly relative to 12% y/y growth in Q1.

To me, there are several indicators that Okta is executing well. Net revenue retention rates clocked in at 106%, indicating that the average Okta customer is expanding their spending on the platform by 6% year over year. On top of this, Okta's remaining performance obligations (RPO) grew 18% y/y, with long-term RPO growing at an even faster 25% y/y pace—which indicates that more customers are signing on for longer-term commitments, stabilizing Okta's business.

Recall that Okta had originally been concerned about sales momentum in certain sectors, including the public sector. Management notes now that these headwinds have not materialized, which is what allowed Okta to raise its outlook back up. Management notes as well that its sales force productivity is improving.

Per CFO Brett Tighe's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Last quarter, we introduced some conservatism in our business outlook with regard to uncertainty in the macro and our federal vertical. I'm pleased to say that neither materialized, and we're removing them from our outlook for the remainder of the fiscal year. While we're only 2 quarters into our go-to-market realignment, we're encouraged by the signals we're seeing, including improved sales productivity and record pipeline generation. This success echoes the long-term performance of our already specialized teams in areas like the U.S. SMB market and public sector. The positive signals from Q2 give us great confidence that our increased specialization will drive long-term growth for both Okta and our customers.

That's a nice segue into the strong Q2 performance we saw in our public sector business. While we did experience some contract restructuring with civilian agencies and delays in procurement processes, renewals across all of federal were strong, reflecting the mission-critical nature of our solutions. Okta continues to perform well with government organizations at all levels, highlighted this quarter by multiple new business and upsell deals with DoD and state agencies.

As a result of healthy top-line performance and careful cost controls, Okta's pro forma operating margins rose 5 points y/y to 28%:

We note that this puts Okta just above the "Rule of 40" threshold. And considering the company's mid-teens RPO growth, we think it's unlikely that Okta's revenue growth will meaningfully slow, and the company should be able to retain "Rule of 40" status for a sustained period. Okta's free cash flow of $400 million in the first half of FY26 also grew 37% y/y, with FCF margins of 28% in line with the company's guidance for the year and improving 5 points y/y.

Key takeaways

In my view, the best thing that investors can do to prepare for a market downturn is to rotate more of our portfolios into underappreciated, undervalued growth names like Okta. This is a company with healthy revenue trends, reassuring backlog growth, and a growing FCF profile. Stay long here.

data.title: Warby Parker Is Still Worth Buying At This Price
data.description:
While many people think of Warby Parker (NYSE:WRBY) as a discount DTC brand, this company is now operating eyeglass shops throughout the United States. It’s not as much of a discount brand as it used to be, either. As an online discount store, Warby Parker was not profitable. But it’s now operating more like a traditional eyeglass store such as EssilorLuxottica’s (OTCPK:ESLOF) LensCrafters or National Vision Holdings’ (EYE) America’s Best, and its margins are rising.

When Warby Parker went public as a DTC company during the pandemic in 2021, investors were excited about a startup with plans to disrupt the traditional eyeglass industry with online sales. Now there’s a different bull case for this stock. Warby Parker is rapidly adding stores. According to its second-quarter 2025 earnings call, it will open a total of 45 stores this year including 5 Target pop-up stores. It had 276 stores at the end of 2024, so it will have 321 stores at the end of this year.

At that pace, Warby Parker will achieve its long-term goal of 900 stores in 13 years, or 2038. While the company’s management talks about their goal of 900 stores a lot, this might be a conservative estimate. The optometry business is highly fragmented. Warby Parker’s management went to a Goldman Sachs conference in September where co-founder David Gilboa said about half of the eyeglass stores in the US are still run by independent optometrists. And the company could also enter into partnerships with other big-box retailers besides Target and set up pop-ups in their stores as well. Of course, I’m talking about events that might happen far in the future here, so this is speculative. But if you’re buying Warby Parker’s stock because it will eventually have 900 stores, you might want to consider that it could expand beyond that.

The reason why this matters is that right now many investors believe that Warby Parker is overvalued. The bear case for Warby Parker is that its stock is too expensive right now even though its expansion strategy is working. In fact, back in August, one of the reasons why I recommended buying National Vision Holdings was that it had a much lower valuation than Warby Parker. Even after National Vision Holdings’ recent rally, this is still true. But Warby Parker has a high valuation because investors believe in its growth potential, so I decided to check if this bull case was justified.

Warby Parker’s Stock Is Still Expensive

According to Seeking Alpha, Warby Parker gets D- or F grades on almost every valuation metric right now. Of course, this is in comparison to other specialty retail stores and not eyeglass stores specifically, and eyeglass stores have higher margins. So I’ll compare Warby Parker to the other eyeglass stores. After the market close on September 25, Warby Parker had a forward EV/sales ratio of 3.82 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 33.57. This is still high in comparison with National Vision Holdings, which had a forward EV/sales ratio of 1.49 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 15.68. And EssilorLuxottica, the global leader in the eyeglass business, had a forward EV/sales ratio of 4.91 and a forward EV/EBITDA of 19.23.

These metrics don’t make Warby Parker a bad deal by themselves, but it does mean that it’s necessary to justify the company’s high valuation. Additionally, it’s important to note that because the optometry market is so fragmented, all of these companies can grow market share by taking business from independent optometrists as well as each other. So I’m not changing my view on National Vision, but I also think Warby Parker can grow rapidly right now.

Warby Parker Now Has Wealthier Customers

Management also went into more detail about how the company’s plans are going at the Goldman Sachs conference. According to co-founder Neil Blumenthal, higher-income customers are still shopping at Warby Parker and many of them are repeat customers. This is important because many investors are concerned about the overall health of the economy right now. Shoppers are cutting back on unnecessary expenses. But if your prescription changed or your glasses broke you need to get new glasses, so this store sells necessary products. Of course, paying for them could be another issue.

While many eyeglass purchases are covered by insurance, insurance cuts are also a concern. For example, Federally Qualified Health Centers provide eye exams for low-income patients and Medicaid cuts will reduce their funding. While Warby Parker still sells entry-level glasses to low-income customers who might be affected by federal insurance cuts, it’s also attracting higher-income customers with insurance plans that can cover more expensive glasses.

By appealing to wealthier customers, Warby Parker has also been able to raise its prices, which has helped it absorb the costs of building brick-and-mortar eyeglass stores. Back in 2019, when it only operated as a DTC store, Warby Parker had a 60 percent gross margin. As an eyeglass store with an expanding physical presence, it has a gross margin of 55 percent in 2024. Its EBITDA is now positive and its net income is expected to be positive in 2026 as well.

Store Expansion Strategy

Warby Parker also plans to continue adding stores in its existing markets. It isn’t worried about cannibalization, and that makes sense. I’ve seen LensCrafters stores in malls in nearby cities before, and of course there are many other independent optometrists with practices in those cities. Eyeglass stores also market themselves based on convenience by promising to make your lenses quickly. During the Goldman Sachs interview, management even explained Warby Parker has added stores in 220 markets in the US, but it only has 1 store in 190 of those markets.

Another factor that some investors may not have considered is that because Warby Parker is growing its store count this quickly, it has more earning power than its financial reports imply. Warby Parker is building relatively small stores; Sachse Construction built several stores with footprints of between 940 to 1,800 square feet for the company. But even small eyeglass stores need to stock frames and lenses, buy equipment, and pay optometrists and other staff. While Warby Parker reported a loss of $20.39 million last year, it also added 41 stores. FinModelsLab estimates that it could cost $485,000 to $1.225 million to open an optical shop. And Warby Parker gives its optometrists and lab employees stock as well and includes those expenses in its gross margin, according to its earnings call. So this company’s brick-and-mortar store model is profitable, it’s just investing in expansion.

Valuation for Warby Parker

If you take the midpoint of its guidance, Warby Parker expects $884 million in revenue and $99.5 million in adjusted EBITDA this year. Its revenue will grow by about 14.5 percent and its EBITDA margin will be about 11.25 percent. It’s also expecting 14 percent growth in store count. So if Warby Parker adds another 45 stores next year, it will have 366 stores. That’s a 12 percent growth rate, so you could project positive comps and estimate 12.5 percent revenue growth. Warby Parker’s estimated revenue for 2026 would be $995 million.

Warby Parker’s EBITDA margin rose from 10.4 percent to 11.7 percent year-over-year in the second quarter. That’s 130 basis points. During the Goldman Sachs call, cofounder Neil Blumenthal explained that the company is trying to raise its margin by 100 to 200 basis points per year and has a long-term goal of achieving a 20 percent EBITDA margin. The midpoint of this estimate is 150 basis points. So you can project that in 2026, Warby Parker will have an EBITDA margin of 12.75 percent and an EBITDA of $127 million.

Warby Parker currently has a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 34. Its EV is $3.38 billion and this includes $228 million in debt, $286 million in cash, and a $3.44 billion market cap. If you divide $3.38 billion by $127 million you get a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 27, which is lower than analysts’ estimate. Warby Parker’s EV would have to increase to $4.26 billion to reach an EV/EBITDA ratio of 34. So this company’s estimated market cap for 2026 would be $4.32 billion.

You also have to consider a company’s share count before calculating the stock price. Warby Parker had 120.4 million shares outstanding at the start of 2024. It currently has 121.7 million shares outstanding. It issues shares to compensate its optometrists and other staff and its share count rose by 3 million from 2023 to 2024. So you can estimate that it would have a share count of 126.4 million at the end of 2026. If I divide the market cap of $4.26 billion by this share count, that’s a price of $34 per share. The closing price on September 25 was $27 per share so this would be 26 percent growth.

SA analysts and Wall Street analysts both rate Warby Parker a buy right now, although Wall Street analysts have a price target of $26. The SA quant rating is hold.

Conclusion

While Warby Parker’s stock does have a high valuation right now, this valuation is justified. This company is rapidly building brick-and-mortar stores and its margins are rapidly rising as well. It has introduced higher-priced products that appeal to wealthier customers, and its customers need the products it sells. It’s also coming up with additional growth drivers such as its Target partnership. So I would rate Warby Parker a buy.

data.title: Wise: High Margins, Strong Growth, And Durable Moats
data.description:
Wise (OTCPK:WPLCF) (OTCPK:WIZEY), a London-based fintech founded by Estonian entrepreneurs, has grown into a major global player. Initially focused on international money transfers, the company is expanding into offering debit cards and a range of other financial services.

I personally began using Wise for cross-border transfers 15 years ago and have been impressed by how much the service has evolved. Transfers that once took days can now be completed in seconds.

At first glance, the valuation may look demanding — trading at around 40x P/E and 17x EV/EBITDA. However, Wise combines two of the most sought-after qualities in an investment: high margins and strong growth.

Its global moat rests on network effects: partnerships with local banks, established AML compliance systems, and a growing recurring customer base. These are not easily replicated. It would not be surprising if global payment giants such as Visa (Visa) or Mastercard (MA), for example, viewed Wise as an attractive acquisition target, especially as its multi-currency account increasingly positions it as a true global banking alternative.

Latest results

Latest results show how Wise has shifted from a pure international transfer payment to a global bank. Transfers still account for 60% of revenue, but interest income has surged to 25% in FY24/25, with cards, accounts, and business services making up the balance. Cross border transfers grow at a single-digit rate, while the rest grow at 25% YoY.

Over the past four years, revenue has compounded at double-digit rates, rising 3.2x. Supported by its asset-light model and operating leverage, profit before tax has surged 7.2x in the same period.

The B2B segment has also expanded, often outpacing B2C in growth. Since 2024, however, overall revenue growth has moderated down from the 30–50% range achieved before 2023. Even so, management remains confident, targeting 15–20% annual growth. This outlook is underpinned by continued customer acquisition, deeper engagement, and the ramp-up of platform partnerships and new business lines.

I am confident that Wise can sustain its nearly 20% revenue growth trajectory. Customers increasingly value speed and reliability, with most Wise transactions now settling in under 20 seconds. As of June, Wise reported 17% customer growth over the prior 12 months, reaching 15.5 million users worldwide.

Partnerships have been a key driver of this momentum. In the last year, Wise has announced collaborations with major global players, including Morgan Stanley (MS), Standard Chartered (OTCPK:SCBFF), UniCredit, and Nubank (NU), expanding distribution to sustain growth and enhancing credibility in these markets.

Equally important is its partnership with Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), which integrates Wise into Google Wallet and Google Search. This move not only improves visibility and accessibility but also leverages Wise’s industry-leading low-cost pricing to attract new customers. It will enable Wise to use Google Wallet and appear on Google searches, which is critical to exploiting its cheap pricing advantage.

Business model and moat

International payments have historically been a lucrative business for global banks, which rely on intermediary institutions through the SWIFT network. In recent decades, strict compliance requirements have added complexity and costs.

If compliance were not such a critical factor, the sector would resemble a pure technology business—potentially a winner-takes-all market with lower take rates. Wise’s regulatory licenses and banking relationships across multiple jurisdictions represent a critical competitive asset. Replicating this footprint would require competitors to commit years and significant resources to meet complex compliance requirements.

By "skipping" the SWIFT network, the Wise model delivers three major benefits for customers:

  • Highly competitive fees (avoids hidden correspondent bank charges)
  • Mid-market exchange rates, ensuring transparency
  • Faster transfers, often within seconds, (improves customer satisfaction).

See below how fast and cheap transfers are made.

Its network, built through partnerships and local accounts in nearly 70 countries, is especially difficult to reproduce quickly, as it requires correspondent banking relationships and a robust compliance infrastructure.

The Wise network includes liquidity and treasury operations across nearly 40 currencies, enabling near-instant payments. With $140 BN transacted annually, the company has driven fees significantly lower, creating a cost structure that smaller entrants struggle to match profitably.

The company has been deliberate in addressing the expectations of smaller customers accustomed to fast, reliable online banking. With a base of more than 15MM users, Wise competes effectively against incumbents like Western Union and MoneyGram, which remain too anchored in legacy models to transition fully to the digital environment (though they provide the service today).

Valuation and peers

Wise is growing at double digits and has an interesting EBITDA margin. Silicon Valley's rule of 40, with revenue growth and EBITDA margin added above 40%, is fulfilled by Wise, with growth near 15% and an adjusted EBITDA margin at around 40%.

Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY), another European asset-light fintech, which I view as a close peer, currently trades at around 10x EV/EBITDA, while its revenue growth is not significantly different from Wise’s. Based on this comparison, I believe Wise could deliver as much as 30%-50% upside purely from multiple expansion plus 20% per year from EPS growth.

In addition, I ran a DCF model assuming a long-term growth rate of 3%, a WACC of 8%. Revenue is projected to grow at a 14% CAGR, with gross margins remaining stable. As overheads dilute with scale, EBITDA margins improve, reaching £1.3 billion by 2030. For terminal value, I applied the average of the last three years to smooth out growth assumptions.

Under these assumptions, the model points to a potential upside of approximately 37%.

Risks

I view compliance and regulatory risk as the major risks facing Wise. So far, the company has managed this effectively, but history shows how costly lapses can be. Competitors with longer track records, such as Western Union (WU) and MoneyGram, have faced multimillion-dollar fines from U.S. authorities. Even large international banks as Standard Chartered, despite strict controls, have incurred significant penalties for compliance failures. With hundreds of billions of dollars transferred annually through WISE platforms, it seems almost inevitable that some illicit transactions could slip through Wise’s systems. Some traditional banks view Wise as having “free-ridden” on their compliance infrastructure that they have invested heavily in, while benefiting from their established networks without bearing the same costs.

Another relevant risk is disruption from new technologies. Western Union and others are experimenting with stablecoins to bypass correspondent banks. While this could reduce costs, it also introduces regulatory uncertainty. I expect U.S. and European authorities will continue to push for tighter oversight to combat money laundering. Any player using crypto or alternative rails will still face the need for compliance and rigorous KYC standards. Over time, Wise’s ability to strengthen its compliance framework could itself become a durable moat, reinforcing trust and differentiation in the market.

Closing remarks

Wise solid competitive moat is built on its scale (network effects), strong relationships with banks, and excellent customer service.

Its higher P/E and EV/EBITDA multiples are well justified by the company’s strong growth trajectory. The rollout of new banking products is gaining momentum and creates a unique cross-selling opportunity.

Any price below USD15 or GBP 1100 represents an attractive entry point. Additionally, while not certain, a potential U.S. listing planned for next year could act as a catalyst to further re-rate the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Entergy: Upgrading To Buy As Meta Approval Reinforces Long-Term Growth Case
data.description:
Entergy Corporation (NYSE:ETR) is up about 10% since I compared it to Ameren (AEE) in May. After rallying in July ahead of Q2 results, we saw another spike in mid-September following regulatory approval for Entergy Louisiana’s 5GW Meta (META) project. With that, the stock has outperformed the Utilities Select Sector SPDR® Fund (XLU) by about 6.5% since the start of the year:

Entergy is now up around 40% over the past twelve months. Is there more upside?

This update reviews Entergy’s expanded $40B capital plan, prompted by the Meta project. I also outline the company’s plans for meeting 8 GW of signed industrial demand, with another 5–10 GW under discussion across the Gulf South. I find the long-range setup promising. The Meta project is part of a broader wave of industrial and AI-related load growth across Entergy’s service area.

While the stock has rerated, I now see a clearer path for long-term growth and am upgrading to a cautious Buy.

Entergy’s Business Model

Entergy serves ~3 million electric customers in Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. It is vertically integrated, meaning it owns and operates most of its generation, transmission, and distribution assets. As a regulated utility, it earns authorized rates of return on capital expenditures approved by state commissions. These vary by jurisdiction.

About half Entergy’s load is industrial. This includes LNG export terminals, chemical manufacturers, refineries, and now data centers. Recent growth has been concentrated in Louisiana and Mississippi, where the company is securing commitments both for new facilities and grid resilience projects. The rest is split between residential and commercial customers. Its portfolio is mainly natural gas and nuclear with growing contributions from solar and battery storage.

As of mid-2025, Entergy had around 24 GW of installed capacity. It has signed ~8 GW of new service agreements with another 5–10 GW of prospective load. Much of this is tied to AI and industrial demand.

Entergy’s Latest Financial Results

Entergy’s Q2 was pretty solid overall, with adjusted EPS of $1.05 beating estimates by $0.14 and rising from $0.96 a year ago. Net income was $468M, and revenue increased 12.7% year-over-year to $3.33B. Total retail sales volume grew 3% on a 12% increase in industrial usage.

Management reaffirmed the full-year guidance and investors responded positively. Base revenue increased year-over-year though O&M, depreciation, and interest costs were higher. Operating returns also improved year-over-year, particularly in Arkansas and Louisiana.

Updated Investment Thesis for Entergy

The most consequential development since my previous article is the approval of Entergy Louisiana’s infrastructure plan to serve Meta’s new data center campus in Richland Parish. The Louisiana Public Service Commission (LPSC) granted regulatory clearance in August. This green-lit an expansive buildout of generation and transmission assets tied to this project.

This will be Meta’s largest data center facility in the world, spanning ~4 million sq. ft. and housing its most advanced A.I. workloads. Once operational, it’s expected to require as much as 5 GW of electricity, making it one of the largest non-utility power users in the U.S. According to Entergy and the LPSC, this site will consume more power than some small U.S. states. Construction is already underway, and Entergy has begun substation and transmission work at the site.

The regulatory agreement permits Entergy Louisiana to construct three combined-cycle gas generation facilities to serve the load. Two will be located in Richland Parish itself and are expected to come online in late 2028. The third will be built at Entergy’s existing Waterford site in St. Charles Parish, with completion by the end of 2029. These plants will add ~2.3GW and are being designed with future carbon capture capabilities.

In addition to gas-fired generation, the LPSC authorized up to 1,500 MW of new solar projects. These will be developed through Entergy’s Corporate Sustainability Rider, which allows large customers (here Meta) to fund renewable capacity to support their energy usage. Entergy will own the assets, but Meta is covering the associated costs.

Meta is also funding 100% of project-specific infrastructure, including some transmission upgrades and various on-site facilities. This structure was key to the Commission’s approval. The LPSC was adamant about protecting other ratepayers from subsidizing corporate expansion.

Management did not name Meta on the Q2 call but cited “substantial” industrial load growth in Arkansas and Louisiana. They raised the four-year industrial sales forecast to over 13%, up from 11–12%. This is a clear growth signal for a regulated utility, especially as many peers are still in discussions with potential data center customers. Entergy already has the permits, regulatory approval, and has broken ground.

The Meta project was the catalyst for Entergy raising its four-year capital plan by $3B, bringing the total to $40B through 2028. This is spread across:

  • 5 GW of solar generation
  • 1.4 GW of battery storage
  • 8 GW of high-efficiency natural gas plants
  • $2B+ in grid resilience initiatives (mostly in LA)

Some of this predates the Meta agreement: Entergy is also working with other large-scale industrial and data center customers across various jurisdictions. Signed service agreements now total ~8 GW of new load (including Meta), with another 5–10 GW of demand under discussion.

Funding for this capital expansion appears manageable. The latest equity settlement in March appears to have met the company’s near-term needs, and anything else through 2028 should be coverable through at-the-market issuances. Credit metrics are solidly investment-grade (S&P at BBB+). Entergy’s nuclear facilities continue to generate tax credits, contributing $571M in 2024, and that should also help reduce the need for external financing.

Valuation and the Dividend

At around $90, Entergy trades at just over 23x the midpoint of its 2025 guidance range ($3.75–$3.95). That’s well above its historical average, and other metrics also point to a valuation premium:

While the current levels look elevated, Entergy’s long-term earnings outlook appears strong. Management raised its 2027 and 2028 EPS targets following Q2. The revised guidance for 2028 is now $5.20–$5.50. If achieved, that would translate to a CAGR of 8.5%.

In short, the valuation screens expensive on a backward-looking basis but appears more reasonable when factoring in growth.

The stock’s rally has compressed the forward dividend yield to 2.66%, below its usual range. But combined with earnings growth, there’s potential for low double-digit annual returns over the next several years.

Commentary on Risks

The Meta buildout includes three major gas plants, solar installations, and expansive supporting infrastructure. The permitting is done, but construction delays could impact the timeline. Zooming out, Entergy has also seen softer residential demand (down 7% in Q2 and 4% on a weather-adjusted basis) and some localized capacity cost issues in Texas. These have not materially affected earnings but are worth monitoring. Regulatory conditions are good in Louisiana and Arkansas, but the political landscape could shift if concerns over large-load industrial users gain traction.

Verdict: Upgrading Entergy to Buy

Entergy’s stock has appreciated quite a bit since May, but I have more confidence in the long-term outlook. The Meta project isn’t a hypothetical (as with many of Entergy’s peers) – it’s approved, funded, and already in progress. It’s evidence of the kind of large-scale demand Entergy has been planning for.

At the time of my last article, I saw the stock as fairly valued. I’m now upgrading to a cautious Buy. Even with the recent run-up, I see enough support for the earnings trajectory to justify starting or adding to a position with a long-term view.

Author's note: This analysis reflects data available as of September 25, 2025.

data.title: Westlake: Painful Moves At The Lower End Of The Cycle
data.description:
It has been nearly a decade since I covered shares of Westlake Corporation (NYSE:WLK) when it acquired Axiall. That coverage has obviously become outdated, but nonetheless remains interesting. Ever since, shares have roughly doubled, including dividends, trailing the market at large, which has tripled over the same period of time.

While Westlake has seen growth over the past decade, it has failed to lose its cyclical element, as the company finds itself at a low point in the cycle at this point in time. Cost-cutting efforts and closing of unprofitable plants might help going forward and might easily drive a cyclical recovery, as the cyclicality makes it hard to create sustainable value in this business.

Performance & Essential Materials

Westlake describes itself as a producer of performance oriented and essential materials as well as building products' producer which focuses on global and low-cost operations.

Operations span over 80 manufacturing sites, some 15,000 employees employed, which generate $11.8 billion in total sales on a trailing basis. These results are reported across two segments, which each report revenues for different business lines as well.

The largest segment is performance & essential materials, this being a $7.6 billion business which posts EBITDA margins around 9% of sales. This business in itself is split up across a $4.4 billion performance business and a $3.2 billion essential business. Activities include product groups like PVC, polyethylene, epoxy and chlor-alkali.

The housing & infrastructure segment is much smaller with $4.2 billion in sales, yet EBITDA margins of 22% make that this much smaller segment actually reports a larger EBITDA number in dollar terms. This is mostly related to housing, and to a much smaller extent infrastructure, with products including building products, including pipes, fittings, roofing, PVC, etc. Such products are sold directly to home builders, or distributed via Home Depot (HD), Lowe's (LOW), among others.

The business generates over three-quarter of sales in North America, complemented by activities across the remainder of the world. The positioning to North America stems from cost advantaged supply of ethane due to shale gas, being key as cheap input to various other processes and products.

Aided by some substantial M&A deals, Westlake has built up quite an impressive growth track record. Revenues have grown from $4.5 billion a decade ago to $12 billion in 2024, having nearly tripled in the period of a decade. Operating margins have been volatile and cyclical, generally having come in between 10-20% of sales. Impressive is that this growth has been achieved amidst minimal dilution being incurred.

All this has been reflected in the share price, which has displayed a lot of volatility. Shares nearly peaked at $100 in 2014, fell to the $40 mark in 2016, to see another such wave pre-pandemic. Thereafter, shares hit a high around $160 in spring 2024. Since that point in time, shares have lost half their value, now trading at $76, near their lows.

Establishing A Base

At the start of the year, Westlake reported a 3% fall in 2024 sales to $12.1 billion. Pressured by notably a weak performance at the performance and essential materials segment, revenues fell a bit. This also manifested itself in a 2% drop in EBITDA margins to 19% of sales, with adjusted EBITDA reported at $2.3 billion. However, such a number does not say that much in a rather capital intensive industry, with capital spending (as well as depreciation charges) trending around a billion per year.

Following this margin pressure, adjusted earnings fell about three dollars to $5.22 per share, as this margin pressure was the driver behind the substantial pullback seen in the share price in the second half of 2024 and early in 2025.

First quarter sales were down over 4%, as the company reported a small GAAP loss in seasonally a softer quarter, due to weakness in the performance and essential materials segments, as the performance at large was rather soft.

Second quarter sales were down 8% as GAAP losses increased, driven by restructuring costs following a plant closure in Rotterdam. This is certainly disappointing, after these activities were bought as recent as 2022 for $1.2 billion. Adjusted losses came in at 9 cents and while this was an improvement from a thirty-one cent loss in the first quarter, a loss of forty cents in the first half of the year compares to profits of $3.75 per share in the first half of 2024.

With 128 million shares trading down to the $76 mark, the market value of the firm has fallen below $10 billion, in part as the company has racked up $2.3 billion in net debt now, with adjusted EBITDA now trending at less than $1.4 billion based on the second quarter performance.

With a $12 billion pro forma valuation, shares now trade at low levels than seen a decade ago, as we find ourselves at a cyclical lows as the company remains cyclical, tied to energy prices. Given the market conditions, even costs saving targets only have a small impact here. However, the company targets to save another $200 million in costs by 2026, which after taxes works out to well over a dollar per share.

And Now?

Given the situation in which the company finds itself, I was surprised to see Westlake announce the acquisition of ACI Compounding Solutions late in the summer. The acquisition involves the purchase of Portuguese activities, involving the production of specialty materials used in wire & cable sectors, with the activities generating about EUR 210 million in sales, about a quarter of a billion in U.S. dollar terms.

This makes that the deal adds about 2% to pro forma sales. Few other details have been announced, as this is truly a bolt-on deal, although that it adds to debt at a rather unfortunate period of time.

Fortunately, the company has a substantial cash position as debt is both cheap and long-dated, which makes me more comfortable with the existing net debt load. Moreover, recent cost-cutting efforts and plant closures likely benefit results from here onwards, but the issue is that some questions can be asked well, certainly on the capital allocation move, such as the epoxy acquisition in recent years.

Believing that a current $12 billion revenue number is largely fair going forwards, a return to 10% operating margins, as seen in the past, could reveal large upside. Even after accounting for $150 million in interest expenses and statutory tax rates, this would still allow for earnings around $6 per share, or more. If this happens, shares are largely a safe bet here, as the lesson in such cyclical businesses is always tot invest when earnings multiples are high (because earnings are modest or non-existing).

Hence, here might be a good entry point for a cyclical rebound, but Westlake has proven to be a difficult vehicle to create lasting and stable returns.

data.title: I See A Golden Triangle To Push Gold Prices Higher
data.description:
Don’t sell just because of all-time high prices

Readers following our writings know that we have long-term exposure to precious metals. As gold and silver’s powerful price rally continues, we have received some questions regarding our positions. We thought it’s a good idea to compile our thoughts into a more current piece, and the result is the article you are reading now.

To set the background, gold and silver prices kept making record highs in the past 1~2 years and have risen far more than the equity market as you can see from the chart below. In the past 52-weeks alone, IAU (our choice of ETF for gold exposure) had a price return of 39.69%, and SIVR (our ETF for silver exposure) had a price return of 42.63%, both significantly surpassing SPY's (S&P 500 ETF) already-terrific 16.03%. These positions have helped our growth portfolios (our allocations will be detailed later) to widen its lead ahead of the broader market.

Such a large divergence from the SP500 index can certainly lead some investors to worry that the rally is overdone and may not be sustainable. To address this concern, our view is ultimately twofold. Firstly, investors – at least fundamental-oriented investors like myself - should not exit a position just because of high prices. Secondly and subsequently, I remain bullish about the fundamentals surrounding gold. In particular, I keep seeing a perfect alignment of macroscopic parameters (what I call a golden triangle) that can support even higher prices. As to be detailed next, the three sides of this triangle include geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and also the market’s expectations of lower interest rates.

Geopolitical uncertainties

As a reflection of the first parameter above, recent data from the People's Bank of China shows that the country's gold reserves stood at 74.02 million ounces at the end of August 2025, an increase of 60,000 ounces from the end of July's 73.96 million ounces. This marks the 10th consecutive month of gold accumulation. Other central banks have also been adding to their gold reserve lately for good reasons in my view. Amid the trade disputes, I expect other central banks to keep viewing gold as effectively a hedge against the risk of a single currency like the U.S. dollar and optimize the foreign exchange reserve structure.

Speaking of reserve structure, I see plenty of room for China to keep increasing its gold allocation. As you can see from the following chart, gold accounted for around 7% of China's official international reserve assets (which are primarily composed of foreign currencies and gold). This is significantly lower than the global average of around 15%. Given the sheer size of China’s economy (the 2nd largest global economy after the US), I expect China’s potential demand already substantial enough to impact the global supply-demand dynamics.

Inflation

Most gold investors are well versed with gold’s role as a hedge against inflation. The high inflation in recent years certainly played a large role in gold price rallies. Of course, inflation seems to be under control per the most recent data. However, I don’t think inflation has run its course as a catalyst for gold prices yet for at least two reasons. First, the current inflation situation remains fickle in my view and can tick up again for many factors (such as trade disputes). Second, if we expand our horizon a bit, the current gold prices are not that high when adjusted for inflation.

As an example, the chart below normalizes the nominal price of gold by the expansion of the M3 money supply. As seen, over the last 50 years, the M3 money supply represented by the blue line on the graph has shown a consistent and significant increase and is a key (if not the key) driving force of inflation in the long term. The gold price, shown by the green line, of course has followed an overall upward trend too in the long term. But the ratio of the price of gold to the M3 money supply, depicted by the red line, paints a different – and more telling – picture in my view. As seen, despite the record gold prices, the ratio remains at a quite reasonable level by historical standard.

Expectations of lower rates

As for the third parameter (the expectation of lower interest rates), leading institutions (e.g., Goldman Sachs) keep seeing this parameter as a near term drivers for bullish price target for gold. I certainly see good reasons behind their logic.

Both intuition and historical data suggest a negative correlation between interest rates and gold prices. For example, the chart below illustrates the correlation between the price of GLD ETF and 10-year treasury rate. As seen, the correlation is negative most of the time in the long run, with an average of -0.235 since 2005. The reason is simple, as treasury rates decline, more and more investors will find treasury bonds less attractive and begin seeking alternative assets, and gold offers several appeals.

This scenario is quite timely under our current conditions, in my view. The elevated interest rates in the past few years have attracted a larger amount of capital towards treasury bonds. As such, I expect each interest rate cut to trigger the exit of a large amount of capital from treasury bonds, with proceedings looking for new investment ideas. As a specific estimate, a Goldman Sachs report projects that if just 1% of individual investors holding U.S. government debt were to shift their funds into gold, it would mean $570 billion of capital flowing into gold. To better contextualize these figures, over the past year – one of the best years for gold, the GLD ETF (the largest gold ETF to my knowledge) has “only” seen a net fund inflow of $12.7 billion. Under this context, I am not surprised to see the report went on to say that gold prices could rise to nearly $5,000 per ounce due to this extra demand (assuming all other factors remain constant) created by lower rates.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, all the 3 factors I mentioned above are ultimately uncertain and can change in opposite directions as I anticipated above. Also, I assume most investors get their gold exposure via gold ETFs (aka paper gold). These ETFs create the so-called counter party risks and charge relatively high fees. For example, the table below shows 4 popular gold ETFs: two issued by SPDR (GLD and GLDM), one by VanEck Merk Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:OUNZ), and one by iShares (NYSEARCA:IAU).
