As seen, their expense ratios vary in a range of 0.1% for GLDM to 0.40% for GLD. These expense ratios are far above the fees charged by large index equity and/or bond funds (with many of them charging 0.05% or lower these days). It could be an important factor for investors to consider. Also note that all these funds are considered collectibles for tax purposes, and the gains can be taxed at higher rates compared to those from bonds and/or equity funds.

To close, the purpose of this article is to caution gold investors – especially those with a long-term horizon - from selling simply because of its share price rise and divergence from the SP500 in the past 1~2 years. Selling (or buying) should not be based on prices, but on fundamental market forces. In this case, we see 3 forces that can push gold prices further up: geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and expectations of lower interest rates. Let me close by showing our holdings. As seen in the chart below, I have a sizable allocation (about 9%) towards precious metals in my growth portfolio. In my growth portfolio, my precious metal exposure is more toward silver. I will refrain from further elaboration here and leave my thoughts on silver for another day. The allocation is all designated for IAU in my survival portfolio. I choose IAU for its lower fees. I don’t trade often and thus don’t find the extra tradability provided by GLD worth the extra fees. IAU is a legacy position before the inception of GLDM. I am still holding my legacy IAU shares for tax considerations, and use GLDM in my new additions to take advantage of its even lower fees.

data.title: Skyward Specialty: Recent Developments Have Improved Its Investment Case
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article some months ago, Skyward Specialty Insurance Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:SKWD) doesn’t hold any specific competitive advantage in the P&C insurance sector and its valuation was not particularly attractive at the time for long-term investors. Not surprisingly, its shares have performed somewhat poorly since my last article, underperforming the market by some margin since then.

More recently, the company has reported its quarterly earnings related to Q2 2025, reporting a positive operating performance, and has reached an agreement to buy Apollo Group Holdings Limited for about $555 million.

Apollo is an insurer based in the U.S. that operates mainly in the Lloyd’s of London market, being focused on underwriting specialty insurance lines through syndicates. It makes a good business fit with Skyward considering that it expands Skyward’s presence in some specialty insurance lines and brings lines, especially related to new economy industries.

Apollo was established in 2010 and is partly owned by management, employees, and Alchemy, offering data-driven and creative solutions to several parts of the specialty insurance industry. Apollo has developed a relationship with Skyward over the past couple of years, collaborating several times, and Skyward believes the business combination provides a good cultural fit. Apollo’s management will therefore continue to run the business after the integration into the Skyward group, showing that the company sees this business as being well-run and wants to make a smooth transition into its group.

From a financial standpoint, Apollo has a very good growth history given that its gross written premiums have grown at a compounded annual growth rate of about 20% since its inception. Currently, Apollo manages some $1.5 billion of premiums in several specialty lines, generating significant fee-based earnings and has a capital-light business model. Skyward expects this acquisition to deliver double-digit adjusted operating EPS accretion in the first full year after the integration of Apollo, showing that it will have a significant impact on increasing Skyward’s position in the specialty market and also to support its evolution to a more capital-light model.

Regarding valuation, Skyward will pay less than 9x EBITDA expected for the full year 2025, being a reasonable price for a high-growth business that has a recurring business model. This acquisition will be made through a combination of cash and shares, with Apollo’s financial investors exiting from the company, while strategic investors and management will receive Skyward’s shares. Skyward has already reached a funding commitment from Barclays (BCS) to finance the cash component through debt, which will increase its financial leverage when the acquisition is completed. The deal is expected to close during the first quarter of 2026, assuming it gets all the required regulatory approvals.

This combination seems to make sense both financially and also from a business perspective, being an important step for Skyward to continue to diversify its operations by type of insurance risk and will also boost its digital and analytical capabilities. Indeed, its strategy has been to invest in technology and advanced analytics capabilities over the past few years to differentiate itself from competitors in the insurance industry, making this acquisition also interesting from this point.

Regarding underwriting profitability, Apollo’s combined ratio has been slightly below 95% over the past few years, which is a higher ratio than compared to Skyward, showing there is some room for the company to improve its underwriting profits ahead. This can be achieved by a combination of better underwriting capabilities by leveraging both companies’ platforms, but also by better claims management, namely by Apollo potentially using SkyBI, Skyward’s proprietary business intelligence platform.

Regarding its operating performance, Skyward reported its Q2 2025 earnings a couple of months ago, which maintained a positive operating momentum as the market backdrop has remained supportive for P&C companies.

However, not all insurance lines are performing well, and the company adjusted its risk appetite considering current market conditions. It pulled back from some casualty lines where loss inflation is a concern for profitability, allocating more capital toward other lines where it sees better returns in the future.

Given this backdrop, gross written premiums declined in global property and construction & energy solutions, while accident & health, agriculture, and specialty programs reported strong premium growth. Overall, its gross premiums increased by 17.3% YoY during the first half of 2025 to more than $1.1 billion, reflecting a supportive industry backdrop regarding both pricing and volumes. Its net premiums earned amounted to $595 million, or 61% of gross premiums written, being a stable ratio compared to the same period of last year.

Regarding its underwriting profitability, Skyward’s combined ratio was 90% in the first half of the year, being practically unchanged compared to H1 2024, impacted by some convective storms in the South and Midwest that resulted in higher claims costs than expected. Adjusted for catastrophic events, its combined ratio was 88.2% in H1 2025, improving by about 90 basis points (bps) compared to the same period of 2024. This shows that Skyward’s increased focus on underwriting profitability instead of chasing top-line growth is on the right path, being positive for underwriting performance in the near future.

Its investment income increased significantly to more than $10 million in H1 2025, up by 53% YoY, supported by higher interest rates as the company has deployed cash generation into its fixed income portfolio and has reinvested securities at higher yields, plus it also reported higher capital gains in the period compared to H1 2024.

Due to higher underwriting results and investment income, Skyward’s operating income increased to more than $100 million in the first half of 2025, up by 16% YoY, while its net income amounted to nearly $81 million (+19% YoY). Its annualized net return on equity (ROE) ratio, a key measure of profitability within the insurance sector, was 19% in the first half of 2025, being a very good level of profitability compared to peers.

For the full year, the company is well on track to reach its guidance of net income between $138-$150 million, and may even achieve a higher level if operating momentum continues.

Regarding its balance sheet, Skyward has a solid position given that its debt-to-capital ratio was 12% at the end of last June, being at a conservative level compared to other insurance companies and gives it flexibility to integrate Apollo during 2026 and increase its leverage position a little bit. Despite this strong financial profile, its strategy has been to reinvest cash flow generation into the company, namely in business growth and higher allocation into its investment portfolio, while historically has not distributed dividends. This strategy is not expected to change in the near term, especially considering that Skyward will have to integrate a sizeable acquisition over the coming quarters, but eventually it may decide to distribute dividends to shareholders in the future, as an important driver of insurance companies’ investment case is usually related to income.

Regarding its valuation, Skyward is currently trading slightly above 2x book value, a much cheaper valuation compared to when I last analyzed it, given that it was trading at 2.5x book value at the time. This derating is explained by a growing book value that was not reflected in a higher share price over the past few months. Considering that Skywards ROE is quite high, I see a valuation around 2.5x book value as being warranted, making Skyward somewhat undervalued right now. Compared to its peers, which include RLI Corp. (RLI), American Financial Group, Inc. (AFG), or ProAssurance Corporation (PRA) its valuation also seems to be compelling.

Conclusion

Skyward Specialty has reported a positive operating momentum in recent quarters and its recent agreement to acquire Apollo Group Holding Limited seems to make sense both financially and from a business perspective, while maintaining a sound financial profile at the same time. Given that its shares have lagged its peers and the overall stock market over the past few months, leading to a much lower valuation than it was trading some months ago, I now see Skyward as an interesting growth play in the insurance P&C market for long-term investors.
